notes.note: Fundi, a town on the Via Appia in southern Latium where Cicero broke his journey; no registry place entity could be bound to it.

notes.anchor: I am waiting to hear about Marius
notes.note: Not the great C. Marius but the impostor "Marius" (Herophilus/Amatius), a freedman who posed as a grandson of Marius and stirred the urban crowd after the assassination; Antony had him summarily executed in mid-April 44. No registry entity matches this pseudo-Marius, so the surface is left unbound.
Cicero's recurring honorific for the tyrannicides (Brutus, Cassius, and their associates), the "heroes" of the Ides; a collective epithet rather than a single bindable person.
The assassination of Caesar on 15 March 44 BC; throughout these letters the single political fact Cicero says still pleases him.

Curtilius, denounced here as a verbero ("whipping-post," a scoundrel fit only for flogging), had got hold of the estate of Sextilius; he recurs in 14.10 in the list of profiteers exploiting Caesar's confirmed acta.

notes.anchor: the consuls and tribunes of the plebs he chose for the next two years
notes.note: Under his confirmed acta Caesar had nominated magistrates years in advance; Cicero's complaint is that the Liberators must now uphold the dead dictator's appointments for 43 and 42 BC.

Renders Cicero's Greek politeuesthai (to engage in public life); he laces this letter with Greek political terms (soloikon, pepoliteumetha, politikotera) in the manner of his exchanges with the bookish Atticus.
